title: Credit Conditions Survey - 2026 Q1
summary: The Bank of England's Q1 2026 Credit Conditions Survey, conducted 23 February to 13 March, shows credit availability increasing for household secured lending and across all business sizes in Q1. Demand for secured lending for remortgaging rose while house purchase demand was unchanged. Lenders expect secured credit availability to increase further in Q2, and demand for house purchase and remortgaging to rise.
key_points: Secured credit availability to households increased in Q1 2026 (three months to end-February), with further increase expected in Q2.
Unsecured credit availability to households was unchanged in Q1 but expected to increase in Q2.
Overall corporate credit availability was unchanged, but increased for small, medium, and large businesses individually in Q1.
Demand for secured lending for house purchase was unchanged in Q1 but expected to increase in Q2.
Demand for secured lending for remortgaging increased in Q1 and is expected to rise further in Q2.
Corporate lending demand slightly increased for small and large businesses in Q1, unchanged for medium-sized businesses.
why_it_matters: Credit availability and demand trends feed directly into the Bank of England's assessment of monetary transmission and financial conditions. Rising secured credit availability combined with expected higher mortgage demand may influence housing market dynamics. Corporate credit expansion across all firm sizes provides signals about business financing conditions. The survey is an input into BoE policy deliberations.
macro_context: The survey provides forward-looking signals for UK credit conditions. Secured credit availability increased while demand for house purchase was unchanged but remortgaging demand rose. Corporate credit availability was unchanged overall in Q1 but increased individually for small, medium, and large businesses. The survey was conducted February 23 to March 13, covering the period to end-February, and may not capture more recent market developments.

issues_found: Metadata PUBLISHED_AT shows 2026-01-15, but source body clearly states 'Published on 09 April 2026' - the actual publication date is April 9, 2026, making this a current document.
Source body was truncated after corporate lending demand section, potentially missing data on Q2 expectations for medium-sized and large businesses.

macro_context characterizes trends as 'signals potential recovery in housing market activity' and 'improving business financing conditions' - these forward-looking interpretations are reasonable inferences but stated more confidently than the source warrants.
No specific numerical net percentage balance values are included in key_points or important_numbers, reducing precision.

human_review.notes: Issues are minor and do not constitute factual errors. The record accurately reflects the source content with appropriate nuance. Minor date metadata inconsistency and interpretive language in macro_context are addressable without human review.

notes: Survey-based report rather than hard economic data. Qualitative net percentage balances reported. Expectations for Q2 (three months to end-May) should not be treated as realised outturns. Discrepancy between metadata date (Jan 15) and article date (April 9); used actual publication date.

reasoning: High-quality official source from Bank of England.
Directly relevant to macro catalysts via credit conditions analysis.
Clear methodology with net percentage balance reporting.
Truncated body may have removed some data.
Survey-based rather than hard data, reducing precision for market impact forecasting.
